Key experiences: David Sheldrick Wildlife Trust (baby elephant orphanage — the most popular tourist attraction in Kenya), Nairobi National Park (the only national park within a major capital city — where lions and rhinos graze with city skyscrapers in the background), the Giraffe Centre (feed Rothschild giraffes), Karen Blixen Museum (the farm made famous by 'Out of Africa'), Kibera (one of Africa's largest informal settlements — powerful and complex), and the National Museum of Kenya.
hidden_gems: Kazuri Beads (fair-trade ceramic jewelry factory in Karen)
Uhuru Gardens (site of Kenya's independence declaration)
Muthaiga Club (colonial-era member's club — Kenya's most exclusive)
Karura Forest (urban forest for jogging and cycling)
must_visit.food_and_nightlife: Westlands and Kilimani have some of East Africa's best restaurants and nightlife. The Alchemist Bar, 1824, and Brew Bistro are iconic venues.

must_visit.name: Diani Beach
must_visit.description: Consistently rated among Africa's best beaches — a pristine 17km stretch of powder-white coral sand backed by swaying palm trees and turquoise Indian Ocean water. The Indian Ocean reef creates calm, crystal-clear water ideal for swimming, snorkeling, and diving. Diani's marine ecosystem is extraordinary — whale sharks, manta rays, sea turtles, and dolphins are common sights. The Colobus Conservation project protects the rare Angolan colobus monkeys that live in the coastal forest behind the beach.
must_visit.insider_tip: The best diving is at Two Fishes, Kinondo Kwa Tinga, and the Chale Island Marine Reserve — accessible by short boat trip
must_visit.kitesurf: Diani is considered one of the world's top kitesurfing destinations — the trade winds create ideal conditions from July–September

must_visit.name: Lake Turkana (Jade Sea)
must_visit.type: UNESCO World Heritage Site / Natural Wonder / Archaeological
must_visit.description: The world's largest permanent desert lake — a vast, startlingly beautiful jade-green lake in the remote deserts of northern Kenya. Known as the 'Jade Sea' for its distinctive blue-green color caused by algae. Lake Turkana is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (Turkana region's broader World Heritage area — 'Lake Turkana National Parks') for its extraordinary geological and paleoanthropological significance. Koobi Fora on the eastern shore is one of the world's most important fossil sites — where hominid fossils dating back 3 million years have been found. The lake is also home to the world's largest breeding population of Nile crocodiles. The journey to Lake Turkana — through spectacular remote desert landscapes — is an adventure in itself.
must_visit.communities: Home to the Turkana, El Molo, Dassanech, Samburu, Rendille, and other communities along its shores

must_visit.name: Hell's Gate National Park
must_visit.description: One of Africa's most unique national parks — where you can walk, cycle, and rock-climb among wildlife without a vehicle. Dramatic volcanic gorges, towering columns of basalt, geothermal steam vents, and soaring vultures define the landscape. Cyclists encounter zebra, giraffe, buffalo, and gazelle on the cycling track. The Ol Karia geothermal power station within the park generates significant amounts of Kenya's electricity — one of the world's largest geothermal energy projects. The park is said to have inspired the landscape of Disney's 'The Lion King.'
must_visit.insider_tip: Hire a bicycle at the gate and cycle the gorge trail — walking into the narrow Fischer's Tower gorge among the steaming rock walls is unforgettable

must_visit.name: Ol Pejeta Conservancy
must_visit.description: East Africa's largest black rhino sanctuary and home to Sudan — the world's last northern white rhinoceros male (now deceased — 2018). Ol Pejeta is at the frontier of African wildlife conservation, combining high-end tourism with cutting-edge conservation science. Home to the Big Five (lion, elephant, buffalo, leopard, rhino), chimpanzee sanctuary (rescued orphans from Central Africa), and vast open savanna. The conservancy borders Mount Kenya and is run as a self-sustaining conservation business.
must_visit.conservation_significance: Home to the last two northern white rhinos (Najin and Fatu — both female). Scientists are using reproductive technology in an effort to save the subspecies from extinction.

must_visit.name: Fort Jesus, Mombasa
must_visit.description: Built by the Portuguese between 1593 and 1596 to protect the port of Mombasa, Fort Jesus is one of the best-preserved examples of 16th-century Portuguese military architecture in Africa. The fort changed hands nine times between the Portuguese, Arabs, and British over three centuries — its walls bear the marks of artillery fire, carvings of soldiers, and graffiti from sailors of many nations. Today it houses a museum with extraordinary collections of Chinese porcelain, Arab artifacts, and Indian Ocean trade history. The fort overlooks the Old Harbour of Mombasa.

budget_tips: Matatus (shared minibuses) are the backbone of Kenya's public transport — cheap, fast, and colorfully decorated. They run between all major towns.
Local restaurants (called 'joints' or 'mama ntilie') serve the most authentic and affordable food — a full plate of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beef stew can cost as little as KSh 100–200
Airbnbs and guesthouses in Nairobi's Westlands and Kilimani are far cheaper than hotels for the same location convenience
The best nyama choma is at local butcheries (mbuzi choma, kuku choma), not tourist restaurants
Shared safari vans to the Masai Mara cost a fraction of private vehicles — book through reputable Nairobi operators
Free attractions in Nairobi: Uhuru Park, Karura Forest (small entry fee), University of Nairobi grounds, and the Bomas of Kenya (modest entry)
safety_tips: Nairobi is a major African city — exercise normal urban precautions. Avoid displaying expensive electronics and jewelry in public
Use licensed taxis or Uber/Bolt/InDrive in major cities — negotiate fares in advance with regular taxis
Nairobi's matatus are safe during the day but exercise caution at night, especially on crowded routes
The northeastern counties (Mandera, Wajir, some parts of Garissa) and the Somalia border region have security concerns — check current travel advisories before visiting
Coastal rip currents at some Indian Ocean beaches can be dangerous — swim in marked safe areas and heed local advice
When visiting Maasai communities, go with a registered guide and respect cultural protocols — always ask before photographing people
Wildlife encounters: always stay in your vehicle in game parks (except Hell's Gate and a few others designed for walking safaris)
Malaria is present in the coast and western Kenya — take appropriate prophylaxis and use mosquito nets
